coredata.dc:title: Intelligent visual detection of OTC enabled by a multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe: Europium complex functionalized carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xia
Zhang, Lina
Bie, Hongyan
Xu, Jun
Yuan, Yingqi
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: It is of great significance to realize ultra-sensitive and visual detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) residues, especially for public health and environmental safety. In this study, a multicolor fluorescence sensing platform (CDs-Cit-Eu) for OTC detection was constructed by using rare earth europium complex functionalized carbon dots (CDs). The blue-emitting CDs (λem = 450 nm) prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using nannochloropsis were not only the scaffold of Eu3+ ion coordination, but also the recognition unit of OTC. After adding OTC to the multicolor fluorescent sensor, the emission intensity of CDs decreased slowly, and the emission intensity of Eu3+ ions (λem = 617 nm) enhanced significantly, accompanying by a significant color change of the nanoprobe from blue to red. The detection limit of the probe for OTC was calculated to be 3.5 nM, manifesting ultra-high sensitivity towards OTC detection. In addition, OTC detection in real samples (honey, lake water, tap water) was successfully achieved. Moreover, a semi-hydrophobic luminescent film SA/PVA/CDs-Cit-Eu was also prepared for OTC detection. With the help of smartphone color recognition APP, real-time intelligent detection of OTC was realized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor nanoprobe
Oxytetracycline
Visual intelligent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe
Detection of OTC in real sample
Preparation of OTC visual inspection test paper
Visual detection of OTC using semi-hydrophobic thin film
Characterization of CDs and CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe
Optimization for the detection of OTC
Ratiometric FL detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu
Mechanism for OTC detection by CDs-Cit-Eu
Detection of OTC by luminescent thin films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071 , No. 51773052 and No. U1904168 ), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No. 222102320446 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (No. NSFRF210331 ), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University ( T2021-2 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of nanoprobe CDs-Cit-Eu and the detection process towards OTC.
(a) TEM image and (b) size distribution of CDs; (c) FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs-Cit and CDs-Cit-Eu; (d) XPS spectra of CDs and CDs-Cit-Eu.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (a), N1s (b), O1s (c) and Eu (d).

(a) The effect of excitation wavelength on the FL intensity of CDs-Cit-Eu in the presence of 5 μM OTC substrate; (b) The FL intensity of I617/I450 at different pH values; (c) The influence of reaction time on the detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu; (d) The photostability of CDs (450 nm) and Eu3+ (617 nm) in CDs-Cit-Eu.

(a) FL spectra of the nanoprobe at various concentrations of OTC; (b) Discoloration images of the nanoprobe at various concentrations of OTC under a 365 nm UV lamp; (c) The linearity between FL intensity (I617/I450) and OTC concentration; (d) CIE coordinate spectra at different concentrations of OTC.

(a) The response of CDs-Cit-Eu to interfering ions; (b) Selective responses of CDs-Cit-Eu to interfering amino acid; (c) The photos of the nanoprobe after adding interfering ions or OTC under 365 nm UV light; (d) The photos of the nanoprobe after adding interfering amino acid or OTC. (The concentration of OTC, metal ions and amino acids were all 5 μM.).

(a) UV–Vis spectrum of OTC, emission and excitation spectra of CDs; (b) The FL decay profiles of CDs with 0 and 15 μM OTC; (c) Absorption spectra of OTC, CDs-Cit-Eu and CDs-Cit-Eu + OTC; (d) FL spectra of CDs-Cit-Eu before and after adding OTC (5 μM); (e) the sensitization mechanism of OTC to Eu3+ in CDs-Cit-Eu; (f) FL decay profiles of Eu3+ ions with 0 and 15 μM OTC.
The paper-based FL sensor was used to detect OTC under 365 nm UV lamp and the smartphone APP was used to analyze the linearity between R/B and OTC concentration.
(a) Preparation of CDs-Cit-Eu thin film nanoprobe; (b) Thin film CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe for OTC detection.
Detection of OTC in actual samples by proportional FL spectrometry (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Intelligent visual detection of OTC enabled by a multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe: Europium complex functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great significance to realize ultra-sensitive and visual detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) residues, especially for public health and environmental safety. In this study, a multicolor fluorescence sensing platform (CDs-Cit-Eu) for OTC detection was constructed by using rare earth europium complex functionalized carbon dots (CDs). The blue-emitting CDs (λem = 450 nm) prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using nannochloropsis were not only the scaffold of Eu3+ ion coordination, but also the recognition unit of OTC. After adding OTC to the multicolor fluorescent sensor, the emission intensity of CDs decreased slowly, and the emission intensity of Eu3+ ions (λem = 617 nm) enhanced significantly, accompanying by a significant color change of the nanoprobe from blue to red. The detection limit of the probe for OTC was calculated to be 3.5 nM, manifesting ultra-high sensitivity towards OTC detection. In addition, OTC detection in real samples (honey, lake water, tap water) was successfully achieved. Moreover, a semi-hydrophobic luminescent film SA/PVA/CDs-Cit-Eu was also prepared for OTC detection. With the help of smartphone color recognition APP, real-time intelligent detection of OTC was realized.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are very powerful and play important roles in the prevention and treatment of infectious syndrome [1] when used correctly. Oxytetracycline (OTC), a kind of tetracycline antibiotics, is a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent that has great effects on the treatment of respiratory and gastrointestinal diseases, which also has a huge positive effect on the growth of livestock. Therefore, OTC has been widely used as veterinary antibiotics and animal growth promoters [2,3]. However, overuse of OTC has led to excessive residues of drugs in food (such as honey, meat, eggs) in recent years, resulting in a variety of diseases, including allergic reactions, bacterial resistance and gastrointestinal diseases, which poses a serious threat to human health [4-7]. Consequently, the residues of antibiotics in food have attracted worldwide attention, and it is of great significance to realize the ultra-sensitive detection of OTC. However, the existing traditional OTC detection methods, such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), cannot meet the new requirements. The sensitivity and specificity of these methods are usually insufficient to detect OTC in real samples, and often require expensive instruments and cumbersome procedures [8-13]. Therefore, developing an ultra-sensitive and facile platform for OTC instant and visual detection is highly in demand for food safety and environmental monitoring.

Among various detection methods, fluorescence (FL) method is always preferred in the field of analysis because of its advantages of simple operation, low cost, fast response, high sensitivity and easy visualization [14-17]. Among many photoluminescence nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are extremely important and have many advantages, such as good water solubility [18-20] and high biosafety [21,22]. Therefore, CDs plays an important role in environmental monitoring [23,24], food analysis [25], photochemical equipment [26], and bioimaging [27,28]. Moreover, most of the CDs can be successfully modified at the molecular level to produce nanoscale composites for the detection of various targets [29-31], including reactive oxygen species [32-34], metal ions [35,36], antibiotics [37,38] and enzymes [39-41].

In this paper, blue-emitting CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using nannochloropsis as precursor. It is worth noting that the use of renewable and environmental friendly raw materials is of great significance to the sustainable development of the ecological environment. Nannochloropsis is a kind of widely distributed marine algae, which is renewable, cheap, and rich in unsaturated fatty acids, proteins, amino acids and minerals, etc. Thus nannochloropsis is an ideal raw material for preparing CDs. Then the CDs were used as the support matrix of citric acid and Eu3+ for the preparation of nanoprobe for the recognition of OTC. Fig. 1 illustrated the synthesis procedure of the nanoprobe and the detection process of OTC. After dropping different concentrations of OTC into the system of the nanoprobe, the FL intensity of CDs decreased continuously because of the internal filtering effect (IFE), and the emission intensity of Eu3+ ions increased remarkably on account of the antenna effect. The FL color of the nanoprobe changed from blue to red after the addition of OTC. The detection limit of the nanoprobe for OTC was as low as 3.5 nM. Compared with the representative literatures concerning OTC detection in the last five years (Table S2), the nonoprobe here exhibited superior sensitivity, which was obviously lower than the minimum amount set by the world health organization (0.1 mg/L) [42]. Significantly, using renewable, low-cost and eco-friendly materials to fabricate advanced functional materials will certainly have a positive impact on the sustainable development of the environment.

Nannochloropsis was purchased from Shandong Wudi Lvqi Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydro chloride (EDC), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), Eu(NO3)36H2O and oxytetracycline (OTC) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (Cit) was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was photographed using a Tecnai-G2-F30 microscope with an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 using 2000 potassium bromide disc technology, and the model was Brooke V70. The surface property of the material was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, temperature gauge 250 Xi). UV–visible absorption spectra were collected using Lambda950. FL spectrophotometer was used to collect data at 380 nm excitation wavelength. The FL lifetime was recorded on Edinburgh fls980 FL spectrometer.

The CDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. 2 g of nannochloropsis was added to 15 mL of deionized water. After stirring at room temperature for 1 h, the above mixture was placed in an oven and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 8500 r/min for five minutes. Then the supernatant was filtered twice using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 20 h. The dialysate was freeze-dried in a vacuum state to obtain the powder of CDs.

Citric acid (Cit, 60 mg) was dispersed in 25 mL of distilled water, after that, EDC and NHS (Cit: NHS: EDC = 6:1:1) were added. After stirring at room temperature for 12 h, 20 mg of CDs was added and continued to stir at room temperature for another 12 h. Subsequently, the above solution was dialyzed for 12 h using a cellulose membrane (MWCO: 500 Da) to remove incompletely reacted substances. The pH of the dialysate was adjusted to 7.0 ∼ 8.0 with NaOH solution (1 M). Then 40 mg europium nitrate was dissolved in the above solution and stirred at 60 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was placed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) and dialyzed for 20 h to get rid of the uncoordinated Eu3+ ions. The powder of CDs-Cit-Eu can be obtained by freeze-drying the above dialysate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: A total volume of 2 mL of the mixed solution of CDs-Cit-Eu (1 mg/ml, 50 μL) nanoprobe and Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0, 1.95 mL) was prepared. Then various amounts of OTC (0–15 μM) were gradually added and mixed uniformly. After that, the photoluminescence spectra were collected (λex = 380 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better assess the feasibility of the nanoprobe, standard addition method was used to detect OTC in honey, lake and tap water. The sample needed to be processed before detection. The water samples were placed in centrifuge tubes for centrifugation to remove insoluble impurities. Diluted the honey sample 50-fold using a buffer, and then centrifuged to remove insoluble impurities. The CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe (1 mg/ml, 50 μL) was dissolved in the buffer (pH = 9.0, 1.95 mL), then 10 μL of treated sample (honey, lake water and tap water) was added to it. Finally, different concentrations of OTC (0–15 μM) were added to it. The FL emission spectra were recorded under the excitation of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OTC visual inspection test paper
ce:section.ce:para: First, the ordinary filter paper cut into the appropriate size and shape was completely immersed in the solution of the nanoprobe, so that the nanoprobe was covered on the test paper. After that, take out the test paper and let it dry naturally. Different concentrations of OTC (0–15 μM) were added to the dried test paper, and shift in color was observed with the naked eye under the UV lamp (365 nm). At the same time, the color was collected and processed by a smartphone to obtain the relationship between the concentration of OTC and color RGB value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of OTC using semi-hydrophobic thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence film SA/PVA/CDs-Cit-Eu with semi-hydrophobic property was prepared according to the relevant literature [43,44] for the detection of OTC. The specific synthesis steps are as follows: 0.576 mL polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 5 %) solution was added into 3.36 mL sodium alginate (SA, 2 %,) solution. After fully stirring for 1 h, 0.024 g glycerol was added to the above solution as a plasticizer. Then the above solution was stirred vigorously for 1 h. Finally, 0.36 mL solution containing 6 mg CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe was added to the above solution and stirred for 1 h. The above solution was poured onto the glass slide, the film can be obtained after drying. The hydrophobicity and toughness of the films were improved by crosslinking them with 0.2% calcium chloride solution. Finally, the film was washed with ultrapure water to remove the residual calcium chloride, then the luminescent film was obtained. In the process of respond to OTC using the film, a smartphone was used to record the discoloration process of the film under the UV lamp (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The activated citric acid was grafted onto the surface of CDs, and then the carboxyl group of the citric acid was combined with the europium ion through coordination effect, thereby the nanoprobe was synthesized. In order to explore the morphology of the CDs, TEM was performed. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the CDs had high dispersion and uniform distribution. As portrayed in Fig. 2b, the particle size distribution range of CDs was between 1.25 and 2 nm, and the average diameter was 1.65 nm. In addition, the FL spectra of CDs were also studied. The emission peaks of the CDs changed with the excitation wavelength, which was likely due to the different distribution of the particle size and the surface trap states of CDs [45,46]. It can be seen from Fig. S1 that the best excitation wavelength of CDs was located at 400 nm.

Furthermore, the chemical composition of the synthesized materials was analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy. As portrayed in Fig. 2c, the peaks at 3412 and 1650 cm−1 correspond to the bending and stretching vibrations of the N–H bond [47], which fully indicated the presence of amino functional groups on the surface of CDs. Because of the asymmetric stretching vibration of the CH bond, a peak can be observed at 2930 cm−1 [48,49]. The absorption peak at 1410 cm−1 may stem from the vibration stretching of CO [50,51]. After the modification of CDs by Cit, the peak of carboxyl appeared around 1718 cm−1. Compared with CDs-Cit, an absorption peak at 1631 cm−1 can be clearly seen in the infrared spectrum of CDs-Cit-Eu, which originated from the chelation of carboxyl group with Eu3+ ions [47]. The above analysis fully demonstrated the preparation of CDs-Cit-Eu.

Moreover, XPS were carried out to further determine the composition and chemical bonds of CDs and CDs-Cit-Eu. As shown in Fig. 2d, three peaks at 284.8, 399.2 and 532.1 eV can be clearly seen from the XPS survey spectra of CDs, which represented C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Compared with the XPS survey spectra of CDs, CDs-Cit-Eu showed two new peaks at 1135.6 and 1165.3 eV, which belonged to Eu3d5 and Eu3d3, respectively. The above analysis also proved that CDs-Cit-Eu composite was successfully synthesized.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, there were four peaks 288.1, 285.7, 284.8, and 284.2, eV in the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which stemmed from CO, CN, CO and CC bonds, respectively [52,53]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), three peaks at 406.8, 401.5 and 399.7 eV can be observed, which can be ascribed to NO, NH and N-C bonds, respectively [54]. In the O1s spectrum, there were three peaks at 530.98, 531.75 and 532.6 eV (Fig. 3c), which belonged to OC, OC and OH bonds, respectively [55]. As portrayed in Fig. 3d, the Eu3d spectrum was splitted into four strong peaks because of the multiple interactions of spin orbits [56-58]. The four strong peaks are 1164.43, 1154.55, 1134.82 and 1124.57 eV, which can be ascribed to Eu3+ 3d3/2, Eu2+ 3d3/2, Eu3+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 3d5/2 [56-58], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best detection result, the optimum condition for OTC determination were investigated, including the excitation wavelength, pH value and culture time. After the addition of 5 μM OTC, the emission spectra of the nanoprobe were recorded under diverse excitation wavelengths. As portrayed in Fig. 4 a, the blue emitting region exhibited excitation-dependent properties at diverse excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission intensity of Eu3+ ions enhanced, but the emitting peaks of CDs becomes incomplete gradually. Therefore, 380 nm was used to excite the FL of the nanoprobe to keep the integrity of the emission peak of CDs, as well as to achieve high sensitivity of OTC determination.

Moreover, the optimum pH for OTC detection by the CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe was explored. The Tris-HCl buffer solution (1.95 mL) with different pH (3.0–12.0) was mixed with the solution of nanoprobe (1 mg/mL, 50 μL), and maintained the total volume was 2 mL. After adding 5 μM OTC, the mixture was stirred, and the FL spectra were recorded (λex = 380 nm). As depicted in Fig. 4b, the largest value of I617/I450 appeared at pH 9.0, indicating that pH 9.0 was the best pH for OTC detection. This is largely due to that the europium hydroxide will precipitate at too high pH value [45,46,59].

Besides, the influence of reaction time on the detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu was investigated. As delineated in Fig. 4c, after the addition of OTC, the maximum of the fluorescence intensity I617/I450 appeared at 13 s, which indicated that the coordination rate of CDs-Cit-Eu with OTC was very fast. Therefore, 13 s was used as incubation time.

Finally, the photostability of the nanoprobe was studied. Upon the addition of 5 μM OTC, the nanoprobe was continuously irradiated by Xenon lamp for 800 s. As shown in Fig. 4d, the FL intensity of the CDs (450 nm) and Eu3+ (617 nm) in the nanoprobe merely unchanged within 800 s. Furthermore, the stability of the nanoprobe was studied after long-term storage. As portrayed in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Cit-Eu nearly unchanged after storing for 3 days, and about 91 % of the fluorescence intensity was remained after storing for 15 days, manifesting prominent stability of the nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal detection conditions, the ability of CDs-Cit-Eu nanoprobe to detect OTC was evaluated by FL titration. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a that the FL emission spectrum of the nanoprobe was mainly dominated by the blue emission of CDs (450 nm) before adding OTC. After the addition of OTC (0–15 μM), it can be clearly seen that the FL intensity originated from CDs (450 nm) gradually decreased, while the FL intensities derived from Eu3+ ions located at 592, 617, 652 and 696 nm significantly enhanced because of the “antenna effect” [60]. OTC containing β-diketone structure is good sensitizer to chelate with Eu3+ ions, the energy absorbed by OTC can be transferred to Eu3+ ions, which undergo energy level transition and emit red light. During the detection process, the nanoprobe exhibited a remarkable color change from blue to red, which was consistent with the photos of the system in the cuvette under the UV light (Fig. 5b) and the CIE map (Fig. 5d). The detailed coordinates of the CIE spectra were shown in Table S2. As demonstrated in Fig. 5c, there was a good linearity between the intensity of I617/I450 and OTC concentration among the range of 0–15 μM (I617/I450 = 0.76894OTC-0.1229, R2 = 0.99601). According to the published calculation method [61], the detection limit (LOD) of the nanoprobe was 3.5 nM, manifesting superior sensitivity to that reported in the literature (Table S1), which was obviously lower than the minimum amount set by the world health organization (0.1 mg/L) [42].

In general, the real detection environment is very complex, and the coexistence of other substances may interfere with the detection of OTC. Therefore, some representative amino acids (Gly, Ala, Cys, Lys, His) and common metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, K+, Cr3+, Na+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ag+, Al3+, Hg2+, Zn2+) were tested to ensure the specificity of the nanoprobe towards OTC.

As delineated in Fig. 6 a and 6b, the interfering metal ions and amino acids had no significant effect on the FL intensity of CDs-Cit-Eu, and the effect of these substance on the detection of OTC by CDs-Cit-Eu could be negligible when they coexist with OTC. This phenomenon may be due to the strong coordinate ability of OTC containing β-diketonate structure towards Eu3+ ions, which can transfer energy to Eu3+ ions efficiently. Fig. 6c and 6d presented the FL images of the nanoprobe in response to ions and amino acids, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for OTC detection by CDs-Cit-Eu
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the mechanism for OTC detection by CDs-Cit-Eu, the UV–vis spectrum of OTC, the emission and excitation spectra of CDs, the FL decay profiles of the CDs and CDs-Cit-Eu were measured. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the absorption spectrum of OTC partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 7b, nearly no FL lifetime changes of CDs can be noticed before and after the addition of OTC, which proved that IFE was responsible for the FL quenching of CDs [62].

The optical properties of OTC, CDs-Cit-Eu and CDs-Cit-Eu + OTC were studied to better understand the sensing mechanism of OTC-induced europium luminescence enhancement. As delineated in Fig. 7c, the UV–vis absorption of OTC showed two peaks at 267 and 375 nm, and CDs-Cit-Eu showed no absorption peak. Upon the addition of OTC, the main absorption peak of OTC shifted from 375 to 388 nm, which may be originated from the generation of OTC-Eu3+. As portrayed in Fig. 7d, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs-Cit-Eu was dominated by the emission of CDs which located at 450 nm. After adding 5 μM of OTC, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ located at 592, 617, 652 and 696 nm significantly enhanced due to the energy transition from excited state (5D0) to ground state (7Fj, J = 1,2,3,4), in which the transition of 5D0→7F2 was most sensitive (Fig. 7e) [63]. Two main reasons may be responsible for the appearance of these emission peaks. Firstly, OTC with β-diketone structure can coordinate with Eu3+ and transfer the energy it absorbed to Eu3+ effectively via the “antenna effect” [64]. Besides, OTC has stronger coordination ability with Eu3+ than that of H2O molecule. Upon the addition of OTC, OTC is more likely to replace the coordinated water molecules of Eu3+, eliminating the high-frequency non-radiative vibration of hydroxyl [61,65]. Furthermore, the FL lifetime of Eu3+ before and after adding OTC was determined to be 96.89 and 121.78 μs at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 7f), which indicated that there was obvious interaction between CDs-Cit-Eu and OTC, and the OTC could transfer its energy to Eu3+ effectively via “antenna effect”.

In order to prove that the nanoprobe can detect OTC in real sample, standard addition method was used to test OTC in honey, lake and tap water. As shown in Table 1 , during the detection of OTC, the recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) were in the range of 95.39–101.74 % and 0.38–2.2 %, respectively, which demonstrated that the nanoprobe had high accuracy and reliability for the OTC detection in actual samples.

Paper-based FL sensor was prepared using CDs-Cit-Eu to achieve the facile, rapid and visual detection of OTC. The filter paper treated by CDs-Cit-Eu emitted blue FL under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, indicating that the nanoprobe was successfully immobilized on the filter paper. Treated the filter paper with different concentrations of OTC, then observed the color change of the filter paper and scanned the color using the color recognition APP in the smartphone. After scanning, the RGB value of the color were shown on the phone. As shown in Fig. 8 , the ratio of R/B and the OTC concentration exhibited good linearity (y = 0.63093x + 0.47739, R2 = 0.98468). The visual detection limit of OTC was calculated to be 4.28 nM. In short, the paper-based FL sensor designed here achieved highly sensitive and visual detection of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC by luminescent thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based nanoprobe can achieve rapid detection of OTC, but the general filter paper may produce interference, and may have a certain impact on the color image acquisition after being wetted by water. To enhance the accuracy of device detection, a semi-hydrophobic luminescence film SA/PVA /CDs-Cit-Eu was synthesized. Under 365 nm UV lamp, the color of the film was blue. Then different concentrations of OTC were added to the film and the color change was recorded (Fig. 9 ). The trend of color change was consistent with cuvette and filter paper, but it was relatively more sensitive. When the filter paper was used to detect OTC, the color of the filter paper began to gradually turn red when the concentration of OTC was greater than 0.5 μM, but when the luminescent film was used to detect OTC, it had completely turned red in the presence of 0.5 μM OTC. Therefore, the luminescent film showed superior FL performance, and also provided new ideas and methods for the rare earth mixed luminescence sensing platform.

In this paper, a multicolor FL sensing platform (CDs-Cit-Eu) for OTC detection was constructed by using europium complex functionalized CDs. After adding OTC, the FL intensity of CDs decreased because of the IFE, and the intensity of Eu3+ ions enhanced dramatically because of the antenna effect, accompanying by a significant color change of CDs-Cit-Eu from blue to red. The nanoprobe exhibited ultra-high sensitivity towards OTC with detection limit of 3.5 nM. In addition, OTC detection in real sample was successfully achieved. Moreover, with the help of chromaticity analysis program in smartphone, OTC visual detection and real-time intelligent detection based on paper and luminescent films were realized.
Author contributions.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Xia Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Lina Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hongyan Bie: Validation, Investigation, Visualization. Jun Xu: Methodology, Data curation, Resources. Yingqi Yuan: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071, No. 51773052 and No. U1904168), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No.222102320446), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (No.NSFRF210331), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122867.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence and fate of most prescribed antibiotics in different water environments of Tehran, Iran
